---
title: "Exercises: Module 10 — Troubleshooting and Recovery"
module: 10
exercise_count: 3
estimated_minutes: 30
platform_tested:
  - macos-14
  - ubuntu-24.04
  - windows-gitbash-2.44
---

## Before You Begin

Complete [Module 10](../modules/10-troubleshooting.qmd) and [Exercises 02](02-exercises.qmd).
You should have `~/rehab-research/` with several commits in history.

::: {.callout-important}
## These Exercises Involve Destructive Commands

Exercise 10.1 enters detached HEAD. Exercise 10.2 uses `git reset`. Exercise 10.3 uses
`git restore`. All of these are recoverable within the exercise — but read each step
carefully and do not skip ahead.
:::

---

### Exercise 10.1 — Enter and Escape Detached HEAD

**Goal**: Deliberately enter detached HEAD state, make a commit, then rescue that
commit onto a branch before switching away.

**Steps**:

1. Confirm you are on `main` with a clean state:

   ```bash
   cd ~/rehab-research
   git switch main
   git status
   ```

2. Find a commit hash to check out (two commits back):

   ```bash
   git log --oneline -5
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script
...
```

Note the hash of the second commit (e.g., `9d4c7e1`).
:::

3. Check out that commit by hash (this detaches HEAD):

   ```bash
   git checkout 9d4c7e1
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
Note: switching to '9d4c7e1'.

You are in 'detached HEAD' state. You can look around, make experimental
changes and commit them, but if you switch to another branch, those commits
may be lost and Git will tell you about it with 'git switch -c <new-branch-name>'.

HEAD is now at 9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
```
:::

4. Confirm detached HEAD with git status:

   ```bash
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
HEAD detached at 9d4c7e1
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

No branch name — just "HEAD detached at HASH".
:::

5. Make a commit while in detached HEAD:

   ```bash
   echo "# Experimental: test detached commit" >> scripts/analysis.R
   git add scripts/analysis.R
   git commit -m "Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state"
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
[detached HEAD a1b2c3d] Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
```

The commit was created but it is on no branch.
:::

6. Use git reflog to find the commit hash of what you just committed:

   ```bash
   git reflog | head -5
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
a1b2c3d (HEAD) HEAD@{0}: commit: Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
9d4c7e1 HEAD@{1}: checkout: moving from main to 9d4c7e1
3c4d5e6 (main) HEAD@{2}: commit: Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
```

The detached commit is at `HEAD@{0}` — hash `a1b2c3d`.
:::

7. Rescue the commit onto a new branch before switching away:

   ```bash
   git switch -c rescued-commit
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
Switched to a new branch 'rescued-commit'
```

HEAD is now attached to `rescued-commit`. The experimental commit is safe.
:::

8. Verify:

   ```bash
   git log --oneline
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
a1b2c3d (HEAD -> rescued-commit) Experiment: test commit in detached HEAD state
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script

On branch rescued-commit
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

The commit is on `rescued-commit`. Switch back to main to continue normal work:
```bash
git switch main
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes

- **Switching to another branch before creating a rescue branch**: If you run
  `git switch main` while in detached HEAD with commits, those commits become
  "dangling" — still in `.git/objects/` but not reachable from any branch.
  You can still recover them via `git reflog`, but act quickly.
:::

---

### Exercise 10.2 — Undo a Bad Commit with git reset

**Goal**: Make a commit with incorrect content, then use `git reset --soft` to undo
the commit while keeping the changes staged for recommitting correctly.

**Steps**:

1. Accidentally commit with the wrong message and mixed content:

   ```bash
   cd ~/rehab-research
   git switch main
   echo "grip_score <- 1 + 1  # wrong formula" >> scripts/analysis.R
   echo "# TODO: remove this" >> scripts/analysis.R
   git add scripts/analysis.R
   git commit -m "asdfasdf fix stuff"
   ```

2. Check the bad commit:

   ```bash
   git log --oneline -3
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
b3c4d5e asdfasdf fix stuff
3c4d5e6 Fix: use z-score normalization for balance index
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function
```

The top commit has a poor message and mixed content.
:::

3. Undo the commit but keep the changes staged:

   ```bash
   git reset HEAD~1 --soft
   ```

4. Verify the commit is gone but changes are staged:

   ```bash
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
On branch main
Changes to be committed:
  (use "git restore --staged <file>..." to unstage)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
```

The commit is undone. The changes are staged, ready to be split and recommitted properly.
:::

5. Unstage and inspect what was added:

   ```bash
   git restore --staged scripts/analysis.R
   git diff scripts/analysis.R
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
diff --git a/scripts/analysis.R b/scripts/analysis.R
index a1b2c3d..4e5f6a7 100644
--- a/scripts/analysis.R
+++ b/scripts/analysis.R
@@ -N,N +N,N @@
+grip_score <- 1 + 1  # wrong formula
+# TODO: remove this
```

The two lines are visible as unstaged changes. You can now decide what to keep and
what to discard.
:::

6. Discard the bad lines and make a clean commit:

   ```bash
   git restore scripts/analysis.R
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

The bad changes are gone. The repository is back to a clean state before the mistake.
:::

**What you learned**:

- `git reset HEAD~1 --soft` undoes the last commit and leaves changes staged
- `git restore --staged` unstages without discarding working directory changes
- `git restore FILE` discards working directory changes (permanent — use carefully)

---

### Exercise 10.3 — Restore Unwanted Working Directory Changes

**Goal**: Accidentally destructively edit a file without staging, then use
`git restore` to recover the last committed version.

**Steps**:

1. Make a destructive change to `analysis.R` — overwrite it entirely:

   ```bash
   cd ~/rehab-research
   echo "rm -rf everything" > scripts/analysis.R
   cat scripts/analysis.R
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
rm -rf everything
```

The file now contains only this line — all previous content is gone from the
working directory.
:::

2. Check git status:

   ```bash
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
        modified:   scripts/analysis.R
```

The file is modified but not staged. Git's hint already tells you the recovery command.
:::

3. Restore the last committed version:

   ```bash
   git restore scripts/analysis.R
   ```

4. Verify recovery:

   ```bash
   cat scripts/analysis.R
   git status
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

normalize_balance <- function(x) (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
filter_complete_cases <- function(df) dplyr::filter(df, !is.na(grip_strength))

...

On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

The original content is fully restored from the last commit. The destructive change
is gone.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## git restore Is Permanent for Unstaged Changes

The destructive change in this exercise (`echo "rm -rf everything" > analysis.R`)
was only in your working directory — never staged or committed. Once you ran
`git restore`, it was permanently discarded with no way to recover it.

This is the intended behavior — but it means you should **always check
`git diff`** before running `git restore`, to confirm you are not discarding
work you need.
:::

**What you learned**:

- `git restore FILE` restores a file to its last committed state
- This is permanent for unstaged changes — there is no undo
- Always check `git diff` before restoring to confirm you want to discard the changes

---

## Summary

You can now:

- Enter and safely exit detached HEAD state
- Rescue commits made in detached HEAD with `git switch -c branch-name HASH`
- Undo the last commit while keeping changes staged with `git reset HEAD~1 --soft`
- Recover working directory changes with `git restore`
- Use `git reflog` to find any hash from the last 30 days of HEAD movements

**You are ready for [Module 11 — Tool Integration](../modules/11-tool-integration.qmd).**
